title: Soft and Wet Conducting Polymers for Artificial Muscles
abstract:
Research News
Soft and Wet Conducting Polymers for
Artificial Muscles**
By Toribio F. Otero* and Jose M. SansinÄena
Humans try to use their technology to reproduce devices
that have been optimized by nature over thousands of
years during the evolution of living creatures. Among these
systems, muscles are elegant devices developed by evolu-
tion to transform chemical energy to mechanical energy
and heat at constant temperature, far away from the limita-
tions imposed by Carnot's theorem on our thermal motors.
Proteins (polymers), small organic molecules, and inorgan-
ic and organic ions in aqueous media constitute the mixed
materials chosen by nature from which to construct mus-
cles. The increase in Ca2+ concentration caused by a ner-
vous pulse (60 to 170 mV) inside a sarcomere promotes
links between myosin heads and actin filaments bridged by
ATP (adenosine triphosphate) ions. ATP hydrolysis drives
simultaneous conformational changes of different myosin
heads, which act as springs, producing macroscopic contrac-
tion and formation of ADP (adenosine diphosphate). The
ATP is restored from ADP by the glycolysis cycle.[1] The fi-
nal result is a molecular motor working by conformational
changes of a protein assisted by the chemical energy liber-
ated by ATP hydrolysis and triggered by a nervous (elec-
tric) pulse. This is the origin of most of the actions involv-
ing mechanical energy performed by living beings. This is
an electrochemomechanical device.
In order to mimic muscles, soft and wet polymers, able to
interact with ions, are required: polymeric gels, polyelectro-
lytes, and conducting polymers are available. The first gen-
eration of artificial muscles was developed by Katchalsky
and co-workers[2] in the 1950s using polymeric gels. A gel
film is located in an electrolyte perpendicular to an ionic
flow generated from two metallic electrodes. Potentials
greater than 20 V promote electrosmotic processes and the
bending of the film. When the current flows in the opposite
direction a reverse bending movement is observed. Devel-
opments arising from the original idea can be followed in a
more recent review.[3] The non-electronic conducting na-
ture of the polymeric gel and the electrosmotic nature of
the local stresses produced by the current lead to slow
movements and high energy consumption.
In the muscles that we are attempting to mimic, every
myofibril feels simultaneously and directly the Ca2+ con-
centration change produced by the nervous pulse. We are
looking for a material able to feel simultaneously the elec-
tric pulse initiator of the electrochemomechanical process
at every macromolecular motor forming the macroscopic
material. In polymeric gels only an indirect connection ex-
ists, by means of the ionic flow in the solution, between the
electric pulse arriving at the electrodes and the conforma-
tional changes occurring in the polymeric gel. We need a
polymeric material that is, at the same time, an electronic
conductor at the molecular level and electroactive. This
guarantees that the electric pulse arrives simultaneously at
each of the polymeric chains forming the material and also
that there is an interaction between polymer chains and
ions during redox processes. Only conducting polymers ful-
fill these requirements.
During oxidation of polypyrrole (PPy) films electrons
are extracted from the polymeric chains, double bonds are
rearranged, and positive charges (polarons or bipolarons)
are stored along the chains. In order to maintain the elec-
troneutrality inside the film, conformational movements of
the chains (stimulated by the incipient electrochemical pro-
cess) generate free volume, which is occupied by the coun-
terions and water molecules arriving from the solution: the
film swells (Fig. 1). During reduction of the polymer, elec-
trons are injected into the chains and positive charges are
compensated. The original structure of the double bonds is
restored and counterions and water molecules are expelled
towards the solution by the electrochemically stimulated
conformational relaxation, promoting a shrinking of the
film.[4,5]
These
electrochemically
stimulated
conformational
movements of the conducting polymer chains constitute a
molecular motor working by expansion to a rod-like con-
figuration during oxidation, or by contraction to a random
coil configuration during reduction. The direction and rate
of the molecular motor is under control of the electrochem-
ical reaction linked to the conformational movements and
this is controlled by the direction and magnitude of the im-
Adv. Mater. 1998, 10, No. 6

posed current. Conducting polymers are molecular motors
able to work under contraction as well as under expansion.
We call this an electrochemomechanical property.
In order to translate these molecular movements into
macroscopic movements, a group in our laboratory devel-
oped and patented in 1992 bilayer and multilayer de-
vices[6,7] and produced a demonstration videotape.[8] Poly-
pyrrole films of area 2 cm ´ 1.5 cm and thickness ranging
between 4 and 12 mm were electrogenerated on stainless
steel electrodes using the method patented by our group to
control morphology and properties.[6] Once the polymer
film was electrogenerated, the coated electrode was rinsed
with acetonitrile and dried, the polypyrrole film being par-
tially oxidized. A flexible, adherent, and non-conducting
polymeric tape was attached to the coated electrode. The
bilayer was peeled from the electrode and used as a new
electrode (2 cm ´ 1.5 cm) in LiClO4 aqueous solutions. As
counter electrode a Pt foil with surface area of several
square centimeters was used. A saturated calomel elec-
trode (SCE) was used as reference electrode.
The oxidation/reduction of the bilayer promotes move-
ments of the free end of the bilayer through more than
360 in both directions, relative to the vertical position.
Anodic currents promote the polypyrrole oxidation and
the film swells. At the polypyrrole±tape interface, confor-
mational PPy changes pull on the flexible tape molecules,
producing an expansion stress at every point of the contact
interface (Fig. 2a). The result is a macroscopic bending of
the device from the vertical (Fig. 3b), the tape lying on the
inner side (concave part) of the curve (Fig. 3a). During re-
duction, the PPy shrinks and reverse processes occur. The
molecular motors develop a compression stress at every
Fig. 1. Conformational changes and subse-
quent volume changes occurring during
electrochemical
doping
in
polypyrrole
films.
Fig. 2. Movements of a polypyrrole/non-
conducting polymer bilayer (c) produced
by the local stress gradients and bending
originating at the interface between the
two layers owing to reversible conforma-
tional changes in polypyrrole during oxida-
tion (a) and reduction (b) processes.

By Toribio F. Otero* and Jose M. SansinÄena
Humans try to use their technology to reproduce devices
that have been optimized by nature over thousands of
years during the evolution of living creatures. Among these
systems, muscles are elegant devices developed by evolu-
tion to transform chemical energy to mechanical energy
and heat at constant temperature, far away from the limita-
tions imposed by Carnot's theorem on our thermal motors.
Proteins (polymers), small organic molecules, and inorgan-
ic and organic ions in aqueous media constitute the mixed
materials chosen by nature from which to construct mus-
cles. The increase in Ca2+ concentration caused by a ner-
vous pulse (60 to 170 mV) inside a sarcomere promotes
links between myosin heads and actin filaments bridged by
ATP (adenosine triphosphate) ions. ATP hydrolysis drives
simultaneous conformational changes of different myosin
heads, which act as springs, producing macroscopic contrac-
tion and formation of ADP (adenosine diphosphate). The
ATP is restored from ADP by the glycolysis cycle.[1] The fi-
nal result is a molecular motor working by conformational
changes of a protein assisted by the chemical energy liber-
ated by ATP hydrolysis and triggered by a nervous (elec-
tric) pulse. This is the origin of most of the actions involv-
ing mechanical energy performed by living beings. This is
an electrochemomechanical device.
In order to mimic muscles, soft and wet polymers, able to
interact with ions, are required: polymeric gels, polyelectro-
lytes, and conducting polymers are available. The first gen-
eration of artificial muscles was developed by Katchalsky
and co-workers[2] in the 1950s using polymeric gels. A gel
film is located in an electrolyte perpendicular to an ionic
flow generated from two metallic electrodes. Potentials
greater than 20 V promote electrosmotic processes and the
bending of the film. When the current flows in the opposite
direction a reverse bending movement is observed. Devel-
opments arising from the original idea can be followed in a
more recent review.[3] The non-electronic conducting na-
ture of the polymeric gel and the electrosmotic nature of
the local stresses produced by the current lead to slow
movements and high energy consumption.
In the muscles that we are attempting to mimic, every
myofibril feels simultaneously and directly the Ca2+ con-
centration change produced by the nervous pulse. We are
looking for a material able to feel simultaneously the elec-
tric pulse initiator of the electrochemomechanical process
at every macromolecular motor forming the macroscopic
material. In polymeric gels only an indirect connection ex-
ists, by means of the ionic flow in the solution, between the
electric pulse arriving at the electrodes and the conforma-
tional changes occurring in the polymeric gel. We need a
polymeric material that is, at the same time, an electronic
conductor at the molecular level and electroactive. This
guarantees that the electric pulse arrives simultaneously at
each of the polymeric chains forming the material and also
that there is an interaction between polymer chains and
ions during redox processes. Only conducting polymers ful-

are extracted from the polymeric chains, double bonds are
rearranged, and positive charges (polarons or bipolarons)
are stored along the chains. In order to maintain the elec-
troneutrality inside the film, conformational movements of
the chains (stimulated by the incipient electrochemical pro-
cess) generate free volume, which is occupied by the coun-
terions and water molecules arriving from the solution: the
film swells (Fig. 1). During reduction of the polymer, elec-

posed current. Conducting polymers are molecular motors
able to work under contraction as well as under expansion.
We call this an electrochemomechanical property.
In order to translate these molecular movements into
macroscopic movements, a group in our laboratory devel-
oped and patented in 1992 bilayer and multilayer de-
vices[6,7] and produced a demonstration videotape.[8] Poly-
pyrrole films of area 2 cm ´ 1.5 cm and thickness ranging
between 4 and 12 mm were electrogenerated on stainless
steel electrodes using the method patented by our group to
control morphology and properties.[6] Once the polymer
film was electrogenerated, the coated electrode was rinsed
with acetonitrile and dried, the polypyrrole film being par-
tially oxidized. A flexible, adherent, and non-conducting
polymeric tape was attached to the coated electrode. The
bilayer was peeled from the electrode and used as a new
electrode (2 cm ´ 1.5 cm) in LiClO4 aqueous solutions. As
counter electrode a Pt foil with surface area of several
square centimeters was used. A saturated calomel elec-
trode (SCE) was used as reference electrode.
The oxidation/reduction of the bilayer promotes move-
ments of the free end of the bilayer through more than
360 in both directions, relative to the vertical position.
Anodic currents promote the polypyrrole oxidation and
the film swells. At the polypyrrole±tape interface, confor-
mational PPy changes pull on the flexible tape molecules,
producing an expansion stress at every point of the contact
interface (Fig. 2a). The result is a macroscopic bending of
the device from the vertical (Fig. 3b), the tape lying on the
inner side (concave part) of the curve (Fig. 3a). During re-
duction, the PPy shrinks and reverse processes occur. The
molecular motors develop a compression stress at every
Fig. 1. Conformational changes and subse-
quent volume changes occurring during
Fig. 2. Movements of a polypyrrole/non-
conducting polymer bilayer (c) produced
by the local stress gradients and bending
originating at the interface between the
two layers owing to reversible conforma-
tional changes in polypyrrole during oxida-
tion (a) and reduction (b) processes.
point of the polymer±polymer interface (Fig. 2b) and the
bilayer bends, the tape film lying on the outer side (convex
part) (Fig. 3c). Under a current flow of 30 mA a bilayer
whose PPy film mass is 3 mg (area 3 cm2 and thickness
6 mm) takes 3.6 s to describe a movement of 90.
This macroscopic device, just like the constituent molec-
ular motors, works under compression (shrinking) as well
as under expansion (swelling), being controlled by the con-
comitant electrochemical reaction by means of the imposed
current: it can be stopped at any intermediate point, re-
versed from any point, and accelerated or retarded by stop-
ping, reversing or accelerating the driving current. More-
over, the device is able to move up to 1000 times its own
weight attached to the free end of the bilayer in a similar
way to that observed in Figure 3.
In recent years the influence of the different variables of
operation on the bilayer movement have been quantified
in our laboratory. The influence on the movement of the
applied electrical potential and current, bilayer dimensions,
moved weight, and electrolyte concentration[9±11] have been
quantified by measurements of movement rate [rad s±1] and
electrical charge [mC] and energy [mJ] consumed during
the movement. At the moment our work is focused on the
stability and lifetime of the artificial muscle and the con-
struction of a solid artificial muscle based on polypyrrole
and a solid polymer electrolyte able to work in air.
The electrochemomechanical property is a general prop-
erty of conducting polymers. Similar devices based on poly-
aniline[12] or polythiophene derivatives[13] are being devel-
oped. Kaneto and co-workers have proved the reversibility
of the property: a mechanical stress applied to an oxidized
polypyrrole film promotes its reduction and a concomitant
current proportional to the mechanical stress. This is a me-
chanochemoelectrical property.[14]
The devices described here transform electrical pulses
into macroscopic mechanical energy, at constant tempera-
ture, by means of electrochemical stimulation of conforma-
tional changes in polymeric chains, working in aqueous so-
lutions containing ions that interact with the polymer
during work. The great similarity to natural muscles is un-
derlined by the fact that they work at between 300 mV and
2 V (vs. SCE), i.e., between 50 mV and 1.75 V versus the
equilibrium potential of the polypyrrole, very close to the
potential range of natural muscles. All this supports the
name of artificial muscles given to these devices, which are
also called electrochemical actuators.
The two key points related to these devices are the
conditions of synthesis of the conducting film and the
conditions of operation. The electrosynthesis of a con-
ducting polymer is a fast way of obtaining a mixed mate-
rial by means of a complex mechanism (electropolymeri-
zation, chemical generation of non-conducting polymer,
and partial degradation of the electroactive one occur
simultaneously).[15] Our laboratory has improved the syn-
thesis in order to minimize the production of non-electro-
active polymer, improving electrochemomechanical prop-
When the system operates under constant current flow,
any variable, such as the electrolyte concentration, tem-
perature, thickness of the conducting film, and moved
weight, acts on the response potential of the artificial
muscle. The system is, at the same time, an actuator and a
sensor fulfilling most of the properties of intelligent mate-

Fig. 3. Movement of a bilayer device constructed using a 3 mg polypyrrole
layer (area 3 cm2, thickness 6 mm) with a steel weight of 1 g attached to its
free end in 1 M LiClO4 aqueous solution under galvanostatic conditions.
The bilayer takes 9 s to move the steel weight through 180 (I = 30 mA).
The bilayer without the attached weight takes 7.2 s to perform the same
movement under the same applied current.
